message:
Add cargo-fuzz harness with 4 fuzz targets

Implements fuzzing infrastructure for the evaluator and supporting modules:

Fuzz targets:
- fuzz_evaluate: Full evaluator entry point with various keyword combinations
- fuzz_hook_input: JSON parsing for Claude Code hook protocol
- fuzz_context: Shell tokenizer/context classifier span validation
- fuzz_normalize: Command path normalization with idempotence check

Initial fuzzing discovered a bug (git_safety_guard-hcj): regex backtracking
panic in normalize_command with pathological inputs like "//////////////////_(rm".

All targets run locally. CI integration will be added in git_safety_guard-7tg.4.

Closes: git_safety_guard-7tg.2

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-fuzz","title":"Implement fuzzing and property-based testing for heredoc parser","description":"# Fuzzing and Property-Based Testing

## Objective

Ensure heredoc detection is robust against malformed, malicious, or unexpected input through automated testing.

## Fuzzing with cargo-fuzz

### Setup

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dev-dependencies]
libfuzzer-sys = "0.4"
arbitrary = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
```

### Fuzz Targets

```rust
// fuzz/fuzz_targets/heredoc_extraction.rs
#![no_main]
use libfuzzer_sys::fuzz_target;

fuzz_target!(|data: &str| {
    // Should never panic
    let _ = extract_heredoc(data);
});

// fuzz/fuzz_targets/language_detection.rs
fuzz_target!(|data: &str| {
    let _ = detect_language(data);
});

// fuzz/fuzz_targets/tier1_regex.rs
fuzz_target!(|data: &str| {
    let _ = might_contain_heredoc(data);
});

// fuzz/fuzz_targets/full_pipeline.rs
fuzz_target!(|data: &str| {
    // Full check should never panic
    let _ = check_command(data);
});
```

### Running fuzzing

```bash
cargo install cargo-fuzz
cargo +nightly fuzz run heredoc_extraction -- -max_len=100000
cargo +nightly fuzz run full_pipeline -- -max_total_time=3600
```

## Property-Based Testing with proptest

```toml
[dev-dependencies]
proptest = "1.4"
```

## Structured fuzzing

Use `arbitrary` to generate structured heredoc inputs with variants (standard/indented/quoted).

## CI Integration

Run a bounded fuzz budget on a schedule (weekly) and allow manual trigger.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Fuzz targets exist for at least:
  - tier1 trigger
  - heredoc extraction
  - language detection
  - full pipeline
- Fuzzing runs for a bounded time in CI (scheduled) and produces reproducible crash artifacts.
- Property tests encode key invariants:
  - no panics
  - tier1 trigger is a superset of tier2 extraction triggers (no false negatives)
  - determinism (same input → same decision) where applicable
- Any discovered crash is converted into a minimal repro regression test.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-goph","title":"Define Go destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"# Go Destructive Patterns for Heredoc Scanning

## Objective

Define Go heredoc matchers that detect **high-signal destructive operations**, primarily when Go code is used as a wrapper to execute destructive shell commands or delete recursively.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on irreversible filesystem/process operations.
- Do not turn this into a general Go security scanner (exclude unsafe/reflection/network heuristics).

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

- `exec.Command("rm", "-rf", ...).Run()` (literal)
- `exec.Command("bash", "-c", "rm -rf ...")` (literal)
- `exec.Command("git", "reset", "--hard", ... )` (literal)

Avoid low-signal matches:

- `exec.Command($CMD, ...)` should not be a default deny.

### 2) Recursive deletion (high signal)

- `os.RemoveAll("/")` / other clearly catastrophic literal targets → Error
- `os.RemoveAll("./build")` → Warning at most

## False positive mitigation

- Require negative fixtures for:
  - benign command execution (`exec.Command("go", "test")`)
  - benign cleanup under `/tmp`
  - code that prints dangerous strings

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines an initial Go pattern set focused on `exec.Command` + destructive payloads and `os.RemoveAll`.
- Includes at least 5 positive and 5 negative fixtures.
- No default hard denies unless the matched operation is clearly catastrophic.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-hcfg","title":"Add heredoc scanning configuration and CLI flags","description":"# Heredoc Scanning Configuration

## Objective

Allow users to configure heredoc scanning behavior via CLI flags and config file.

## CLI Flags

```
dcg check [OPTIONS]

Heredoc Scanning:
    --heredoc-scan          Enable heredoc scanning (default: true)
    --no-heredoc-scan       Disable heredoc scanning
    --heredoc-timeout <MS>  Timeout for heredoc analysis (default: 50)
    --heredoc-languages <L> Languages to scan (default: all)
                            Values: python,bash,javascript,typescript,ruby,perl
```

## Config File (~/.config/dcg/config.toml)

```toml
[heredoc]
enabled = true
timeout_ms = 50
languages = ["python", "bash", "javascript", "typescript", "ruby", "perl"]

# Graceful degradation
fallback_on_parse_error = true  # Allow if parsing fails
fallback_on_timeout = true       # Allow if timeout exceeded

# Logging
log_heredoc_detection = false    # Log when heredocs detected
log_pattern_matches = false      # Log which patterns matched
```

## Pack Integration

Add heredoc scanning as a pack that can be enabled/disabled:

```rust
// In packs/heredoc.rs
pub fn create_pack() -> Pack {
    Pack {
        id: "heredoc".to_string(),
        name: "Heredoc Scanning",
        description: "Detect destructive patterns in inline scripts and heredocs",
        keywords: &["<<", "-c", "-e", "python", "bash", "node"],
        safe_patterns: vec![],
        destructive_patterns: vec![],  // Patterns defined per-language
    }
}
```

## Environment Variables

- `DCG_HEREDOC_ENABLED=true|false`
- `DCG_HEREDOC_TIMEOUT=50`
- `DCG_HEREDOC_LANGUAGES=python,bash,javascript`

## Default Behavior

- Heredoc scanning: ENABLED by default
- Timeout: 50ms
- Languages: All supported
- Fallback: Allow on error (fail-open for usability)

## Test Cases

- Verify --no-heredoc-scan skips heredoc detection
- Verify timeout is respected
- Verify language filtering works
- Verify config file is loaded correctly
- Verify environment variables override config

## Acceptance Criteria

- CLI flags and config settings map to a single internal config model (no drift).
- Hook mode behavior is safe:
  - timeout/parse failures fail-open (allow) with optional diagnostics.
- `dcg explain` surfaces heredoc config decisions (enabled/disabled/timeout) for debugging.
- Unit tests cover flag parsing + config precedence (CLI > env > config file > defaults).

+{"id":"git_safety_guard-hcj","title":"Fix regex backtracking panic in normalize_command (discovered by fuzzing)","description":"## Summary

Fuzzer discovered a panic when `normalize_command` processes inputs with many consecutive `/` characters.

## Reproduction

```
//////////////////_(rm
```

or bytes with mixed `/` and null characters:
```
[47, 47, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 0, 47, 47, 0, 0, 47, 0, 91, ...]
```

## Root Cause

In `src/packs/mod.rs:490`, `normalize_command` uses:

```rust
PATH_NORMALIZER.replace(cmd, "$1")
```

which internally calls `.unwrap()` on the regex result. When the regex engine hits its backtrack limit, this panics:

```
thread 'panicked at fancy-regex-0.14.0/src/lib.rs:1073:45:
called Result::unwrap() on an Err value: RuntimeError(BacktrackLimitExceeded)
```

## Fix

Handle the error gracefully by returning the original command unchanged:

```rust
pub fn normalize_command(cmd: &str) -> Cow<'_, str> {
    if !cmd.starts_with('/') {
        return Cow::Borrowed(cmd);
    }
    // Handle regex errors gracefully (fail-open)
    PATH_NORMALIZER.replace(cmd, "$1").unwrap_or(Cow::Borrowed(cmd))
}
```

## Artifacts

- `fuzz/artifacts/fuzz_evaluate/minimized-from-6116856d9c40a74797b32a4ad3efe166a30f82a7`
- `fuzz/artifacts/fuzz_normalize/minimized-from-c5521321fb46097edf17593ccf4ac7d3c9fed259`","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-01-08T00:54:15.525383593-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T00:54:15.525383593-05:00"}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-jfj","title":"Design language detection heuristics","description":"# Design: Language Detection Heuristics for Embedded Payloads (Heredoc + Inline Script)

## Objective
Define deterministic, performant heuristics to infer the language of extracted embedded payloads (heredocs / here-strings / inline `-c`/`-e` code), enabling AST-aware scanning *without* introducing false-positive flapping or expensive multi-grammar parsing by default.

This spec is self-contained and intended to directly drive `git_safety_guard-du4` (implementation).

---

## Inputs
Language detection operates on a **payload** produced by Tier 2 extraction (`git_safety_guard-o15`):

- `payload_kind`: `Heredoc | HereString | InlineArg`
- `command_context`: original command string + optional lightweight tokenization of the first line
- `payload_text`: extracted bytes (bounded) and optional `content_preview`
- `redirection_context` (best-effort): any `> file.ext` or `cat > file.ext` patterns near the heredoc operator
- `delimiter_token` (for heredocs): e.g., `EOF`, `PY`, `SQL`, etc.

---

## Output Model

Return a structured result:

- `language`: enum (e.g., `Shell`, `Python`, `JavaScript`, `TypeScript`, `Ruby`, `Perl`, `SQL`, `Unknown`)
- `confidence`: `High | Medium | Low`
- `evidence`: ordered list of `{source, detail}` for explainability

Where:
- **High** = safe to run a single AST grammar for Tier 3.
- **Medium** = run AST only if under remaining time budget; otherwise fall back.
- **Low** = do not AST-parse by default in hook mode; fail-open and record diagnostic.

---

## Deterministic Priority Order (Signals)

We must avoid “random” outcomes. Apply the first applicable rule in this exact order.

### 1) Inline-script interpreter + flag (Highest confidence)
Applicable to `InlineArg` payloads.

If the payload was extracted from:
- `python -c ...` / `python3 -c ...` → `Python` (High)
- `bash -c ...` / `sh -c ...` / `zsh -c ...` → `Shell` (High)
- `node -e ...` / `node -p ...` / `nodejs -e ...` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `ruby -e ...` → `Ruby` (High)
- `perl -e ...` → `Perl` (High)

Rationale: the user explicitly chose an interpreter.

### 2) Executing command prefix (High confidence when command is a known interpreter / REPL)
Applicable to heredoc/here-string payloads when the command that **receives stdin** is known.

We need a best-effort “receiver” detection:
- Prefer token 0 after optional `sudo`, `env VAR=...`, and leading assignments (`FOO=bar cmd ...`).
- If a simple pipeline exists and we can cheaply detect a receiver (e.g., `... | python`), we may use the **rightmost known interpreter** as receiver (Medium).

Mapping table (initial):
- `python`, `python3`, `python2` → `Python`
- `bash`, `sh`, `zsh`, `ksh` → `Shell`
- `node`, `nodejs` → `JavaScript`
- `ruby`, `irb` → `Ruby`
- `perl` → `Perl`
- `psql` → `SQL` (PostgreSQL dialect)
- `mysql` → `SQL` (MySQL dialect)
- `sqlite3` → `SQL` (SQLite dialect)
- `mongosh`, `mongo` → `JavaScript` (mongo shell scripting)
- `redis-cli` → `Redis` (redis protocol commands; treat as `Unknown` for AST, but allow a targeted matcher set)

Confidence:
- If receiver is direct command prefix: High.
- If receiver inferred via pipe: Medium.

### 3) File extension / redirection context (Medium→High)
If we have a nearby redirection target with an extension:

- `*.py` → `Python` (High)
- `*.sh` → `Shell` (High)
- `*.js` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `*.ts` → `TypeScript` (High)
- `*.rb` → `Ruby` (High)
- `*.pl` → `Perl` (High)
- `*.sql` → `SQL` (High)

If extension is found but extraction did not confirm that redirection applies to this payload, downgrade to Medium.

### 4) Shebang in payload (Medium→High)
If the payload begins with a shebang in the first 2 lines:

- `#!/usr/bin/env python*` or `#!/usr/bin/python*` → `Python` (High)
- `#!/bin/bash` / `#!/bin/sh` / `#!/usr/bin/env bash` → `Shell` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/node` / `#!/usr/bin/env node` → `JavaScript` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/ruby` / `#!/usr/bin/env ruby` → `Ruby` (High)
- `#!/usr/bin/perl` / `#!/usr/bin/env perl` → `Perl` (High)

If shebang conflicts with a strong receiver (Rule 2 High), prefer:
- `InlineArg` interpreter (Rule 1) always wins.
- Otherwise: if receiver is `cat`/`tee`/`printf` (non-interpreter), shebang wins.
- If receiver is an interpreter (python/bash/node), receiver wins unless shebang is also consistent.

### 5) Delimiter naming heuristics (Low→Medium)
If delimiter token strongly signals language:
- `PY`, `PYTHON` → Python
- `JS`, `NODE` → JavaScript
- `TS` → TypeScript
- `RB`, `RUBY` → Ruby
- `PL`, `PERL` → Perl
- `SQL`, `PSQL`, `POSTGRES` → SQL

This is never High confidence on its own (users often choose arbitrary delimiters).

### 6) Content heuristics (bounded prefix scan) (Low→Medium)
Scan only a small prefix:
- First `N_LINES=20` and `N_BYTES=2048` (whichever first), ignoring leading whitespace.

Heuristic signals (examples):
- Python: `^\s*(import|from)\s+`, `^\s*def\s+`, `^\s*class\s+`, `print\(`
- Shell: `^\s*(set -e|set -eux|export\s+)`, `\b(apt-get|yum|brew)\b`, `\b(curl|wget)\b` (careful: can occur in other languages)
- JS/TS: `\b(require\(|module\.exports|export\s+)`, `\bconst\b`, `\bfunction\b`, `=>`
- Ruby: `^\s*def\s+`, `^\s*end\s*$`, `\brequire\b`
- Perl: `^\s*use\s+strict;`, `^\s*my\s+\$`, `\bsub\b`
- SQL: `(?i)\b(SELECT|INSERT|UPDATE|DELETE|DROP|TRUNCATE|ALTER|CREATE)\b`

Policy:
- If exactly one language has multiple strong hits → Medium.
- If multiple languages match → Low.

### 7) Config override (Deterministic)
Allow explicit configuration to override detection:
- `default_language = "python"` for unknown payloads
- `command_language_map = { "mytool" = "python" }`
- `project_overrides` keyed by repo path prefix

Override applies at the end (or earlier if configured as “force”). The override mechanism itself must be deterministic.

---

## Ambiguity Handling + Hook Policy

### Default behavior (hook mode)
- `High`: run Tier 3 AST scan for that language.
- `Medium`: run Tier 3 AST scan **only if remaining time budget** is sufficient; else fail-open with diagnostic `language_confidence_medium_budget_exhausted`.
- `Low`/`Unknown`: **fail-open** (allow) and attach diagnostic `language_unknown` / `language_ambiguous`.

### Optional strict mode (opt-in)
A future config mode may allow:
- trying up to 2 languages (e.g., JS then TS)
- or falling back to language-agnostic high-signal patterns only

Strict mode is explicitly not default due to performance + false-positive risk.

---

## Diagnostic / Explain Fields

Return evidence chain to support `dcg explain`:
- `source=inline_flag`, `detail="python -c"`
- `source=receiver`, `detail="psql"`
- `source=shebang`, `detail="#!/usr/bin/env bash"`
- `source=extension`, `detail="/tmp/script.py"`
- `source=content_hint`, `detail="import ..."`

---

## Test Matrix (minimum)

### 10 positive identifications
1. `python -c 'print(1)'` → Python High (inline)
2. `bash -c 'rm -rf /'` → Shell High (inline)
3. `node -e 'process.exit(1)'` → JS High (inline)
4. `ruby -e 'puts 1'` → Ruby High (inline)
5. `perl -e 'print 1'` → Perl High (inline)
6. `python <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python High (receiver)
7. `psql <<SQL\nDROP TABLE t;\nSQL` → SQL High (receiver)
8. `cat > /tmp/x.py <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python High (extension)
9. `cat <<EOF\n#!/usr/bin/env bash\necho hi\nEOF` → Shell High (shebang)
10. `mongosh <<EOF\ndb.dropDatabase()\nEOF` → JS High (receiver)

### 5 ambiguous/unknown cases
A. `cat <<EOF\nrm -rf /\nEOF` → Unknown/Low (cat is not interpreter; content ambiguous) → fail-open
B. `cat <<EOF\nDROP TABLE t;\nEOF` → SQL Medium (content hint), but if multiple hints, Low
C. `cat > /tmp/x <<EOF\nprint(1)\nEOF` (no ext) → Medium via content
D. Mixed content: `cat <<EOF\nimport os\nrm -rf /\nEOF` → Low (multi-language)
E. Pipeline inferred receiver: `cat <<EOF | python\nprint(1)\nEOF` → Python Medium (pipe inference)

---

## Acceptance Criteria Checklist
- [ ] Deterministic priority order defined.
- [ ] Confidence model + hook-mode fail-open policy defined.
- [ ] Mapping table included.
- [ ] Bounded content heuristic scan defined.
- [ ] Test matrix includes ≥10 positive + ≥5 ambiguous cases.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mcf","title":"Define JavaScript/Node destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define AST-aware matchers for JavaScript/Node heredoc bodies that detect **high-signal destructive operations** with minimal false positives.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on operations that are clearly destructive/irreversible.
- Do not turn this into a generic “JS security” scanner (avoid network/exfiltration heuristics).

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Recursive filesystem deletion (high signal)

- `fs.rmSync(path, { recursive: true, force: true })`
- `fs.rmSync(path, { recursive: true })`
- `fs.rmdirSync(path, { recursive: true })`

Severity guidance:

- `Error` only for clearly catastrophic literal targets (e.g., `"/"` or other policy-defined high-risk literals).
- Otherwise `Warning` by default.

### 2) Process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

- `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")` (literal)
- `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])` (literal)
- `exec("git reset --hard ...")` (literal)

Important:

- Do not flag `require('child_process')` alone.
- Do not flag `exec($VAR)` as a deny; it’s too common. At most warn if combined with other signals.

### 3) Known destructive package usage (optional / careful)

- `rimraf(...)` (common in builds) should likely be `Warning` unless literal catastrophic path.

## False positive mitigation

- Require negative fixtures that include common non-destructive uses:
  - `execSync("git status")`
  - `fs.rmSync("./dist", { recursive: true })` (should be warning at most)
  - code that merely prints dangerous strings

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines a JS/Node pattern set that is high-signal and severity-tagged.
- Includes at least:
  - 5 positive fixtures (must match)
  - 5 negative fixtures (must not match)
- No matcher becomes a default hard deny unless it is clearly catastrophic.
- Explicitly documents which constructs are intentionally not matched to preserve trust.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-mvh","title":"Define Ruby destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define AST-aware matchers for destructive operations in Ruby heredoc bodies, focusing on high-signal runtime behavior.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on destructive filesystem/process operations.
- Avoid broad “metaprogramming danger” patterns (e.g., `eval`) as default denies; they are common and not necessarily destructive.

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Filesystem deletion (high signal)

- `FileUtils.rm_rf(path)` / `FileUtils.remove_dir(path, true)`
- `File.delete(path)` / `File.unlink(path)`

Severity guidance:

- `Error` only for catastrophic literal targets.
- Otherwise default `Warning`.

### 2) Process execution with destructive payload (high signal)

- backticks with literal destructive payload: `` `rm -rf ...` ``
- `system("rm -rf ...")`
- `exec("git reset --hard ...")`

Important:

- Do not flag `system($VAR)` as deny; too broad. At most warn if combined with other signals.

## Testing requirements

- Positive fixtures for:
  - `FileUtils.rm_rf("/")` (catastrophic)
  - `system("rm -rf /")`
- Negative fixtures for:
  - `FileUtils.rm_rf("./tmp")` (warning at most)
  - `puts "rm -rf"`

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines a Ruby pattern set aligned with severity taxonomy and focused on irreversible operations.
- Includes at least 5 positive and 5 negative fixtures.
- No default hard denies unless clearly catastrophic.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-t8x","title":"Epic: False Positive Immunity (Execution Context)","description":"# Epic: False Positive Immunity (Execution Context)

## Why this epic exists

A destructive-command guard must be *trusted*. Repeated false positives destroy velocity and lead users to disable the guard entirely.

The core failure mode is **context blindness**: matching dangerous substrings in places where they are clearly *data*, not *executed code*.

Examples that must be allowed:

- `bd create --description="This pattern blocks rm -rf"`
- `git commit -m "Fix git reset --hard detection"`
- `echo "example: git push --force"`
- `rg -n "rm -rf" src/main.rs`

Currently, a purely regex-based guard will often match `rm -rf` (or similar) inside quoted strings and block legitimate documentation/testing work.

## Key idea

Introduce an **Execution Context layer** that classifies which parts of the command line are actually executed vs merely data.

Then, apply destructive patterns only to executable contexts:

- Executed command words and arguments
- Pipe targets (right side of `|`)
- Command substitution (`$(...)`, backticks)
- Inline interpreter strings (`bash -c`, `python -c`, `node -e`, etc.)
- Heredoc bodies (handled by the heredoc epic)

…and **skip** matching for clearly data-only contexts:

- Option values for known “documentation/metadata” commands (Beads `bd`, git commit messages, etc.)
- Grep/Ripgrep pattern arguments
- Echo/printf arguments

## Goals

1. **Dramatically reduce false positives** for doc/test workflows while preserving true-positive blocks for real execution.
2. **Keep performance high:** the non-risky path should stay microseconds.
3. **Provide explainability hooks:** when a command is allowed due to data-context detection, we should be able to explain why (for debugging).

## Architectural constraints

- This layer runs before pack regex evaluation and before AST/heredoc scanning.
- It must be conservative: if context is ambiguous, treat as executable (or escalate to deeper analysis), but avoid blocking obvious documentation strings.

## Success Criteria

- The examples in “Why this epic exists” are allowed.
- Real executions are still blocked:
  - `bash -c "rm -rf /"`
  - `echo hi | bash -c "rm -rf /"`
  - `python -c "import os; os.system('rm -rf /')"`
- Context classification has regression tests for:
  - quoting (single/double/escaped)
  - pipes
  - command substitution
  - xargs
  - interpreter flags (-c/-e/-p)

## Notes for future self

- If we add a “warn mode” or confidence scoring, this epic provides the context signals.
- Don’t overfit to specific tools; start with a minimal safe-string-arg registry and expand based on real false positives.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-t8x.1","title":"Define Safe String-Argument Registry v1 (commands + flags)","description":"## Objective

Create a **versioned** registry of commands whose arguments are predominantly *data*, not code, so we can suppress false positives without compromising safety.

## Background

Many developer workflows involve writing *about* dangerous commands:

- issue trackers (`bd create --description=...`)
- git commit/tag messages (`git commit -m ...`)
- search tools (`rg -e "rm -rf" ...`)
- printing examples (`echo "git reset --hard"`)

Without recognizing these contexts, regex matching will cause velocity-destroying false positives.

## Scope (v1)

Start with a small, high-value registry:

### Always-data commands

- `echo`, `printf`

### Beads CLI

- `bd create`: `--description`, `--title`, `--notes`
- `bd update`: `--description`, `--title`, `--notes`

### Git messages

- `git commit -m`, `git commit --message`
- `git tag -m`, `git tag --message`
- `git notes add -m`

### Search tools

- `grep` pattern args (`-e`, `--regexp`, `-F`, `--fixed-strings`)
- `rg` pattern args (`-e`, `--regexp`, `--fixed-strings`)

### Optional candidates (add only with tests)

- `sed` scripts in `-e` (can be code; treat as code, not data)
- `awk` programs (code; treat as code)

## Design requirements

- Registry must be **explicit and test-driven**.
- Registry must be conservative: only suppress matching where we are confident strings are not executed.
- Provide a way to extend the registry via config in the future (but do not over-engineer).

## Acceptance criteria

- Add unit tests for each registry entry demonstrating at least one previously false-positive case that is now allowed.
- Add at least one counterexample test per category to ensure we do not suppress *real* execution (e.g., `bash -c "rm -rf /"` must still be evaluated).

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-t8x.2","title":"Implement execution-context classification (pipes, subst, quoting)","description":"## Objective

Implement a fast execution-context classifier that identifies which parts of a command line are executed vs data.

## Background

A safe-string registry alone is insufficient. We must correctly classify contexts such as:

- pipes: `echo hi | bash -c "..."`
- command substitution: `$(dangerous command)`
- backticks: `` `dangerous command` ``
- xargs: `printf '%s\n' arg1 arg2 | xargs cmd`
- inline interpreter flags: `bash -c`, `python -c`, `node -e`, etc.

These contexts are **executable** even if they appear inside quotes.

## SpanKind Enum Definition

```rust
/// Classification of a command-line span
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
pub enum SpanKind {
    /// Command word or unquoted argument - fully executed
    Executed,

    /// Quoted argument to a known-safe command (e.g., git commit -m "...")
    Argument,

    /// Content of -c/-e flags (bash -c, python -c, node -e)
    /// Must be scanned for destructive patterns
    InlineCode,

    /// Single-quoted string - no variable substitution possible
    /// Always safe to skip pattern matching
    Data,

    /// Heredoc body - escalate to Tier 2/3 analysis
    HeredocBody,

    /// Ambiguous context - treat as Executed (conservative)
    Unknown,
}
```

## Conservative Design Principle

**"Ambiguous → Executed"**

If classification is uncertain, we MUST NOT suppress pattern matching. The priority order is:

1. **Correctness over convenience**: Never allow a destructive command due to misclassification
2. **False positives over false negatives**: A blocked safe command is annoying; an allowed destructive command is catastrophic
3. **Explicit over implicit**: Only suppress matching for contexts we are 100% confident are data

## Quoting Rules

| Quote Type | Contains `$()` or backticks? | Classification |
|------------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Single quotes (`'...'`) | N/A (no expansion) | `Data` |
| Double quotes (`"..."`) | No | `Argument` (if safe cmd) or `Unknown` |
| Double quotes (`"..."`) | Yes | `InlineCode` or `Unknown` |
| Unquoted | - | `Executed` |

## Approach

### Phase 1 (minimal, high ROI)

- Tokenize the shell command line with a small, purpose-built tokenizer:
  - recognize single quotes, double quotes, backslash escapes
  - recognize operators: `|`, `&&`, `||`, `;`, `$(...)`, backticks
  - identify the first word of each pipeline segment as the executed command

### Phase 2 (harder cases)

- Recognize `xargs` invocation patterns where subsequent tokens become executed commands.
- Recognize `env VAR=... cmd` wrappers.
- Recognize `sudo` prefix.

### Phase 3 (optional)

- Replace tokenizer with tree-sitter-bash if maintenance burden becomes high.

## Output Model

Produce a lightweight structure:

```rust
pub struct CommandSpans {
    spans: Vec<Span>,
}

pub struct Span {
    pub kind: SpanKind,
    pub byte_range: std::ops::Range<usize>,
}
```

The decision engine applies pack regexes only to `Executed`/`InlineCode`/`HeredocBody` spans (never to pure `Data`).

## Safety Rules

1. Single quotes are ALWAYS `Data` - no exceptions
2. Double quotes with command substitution are ALWAYS `Unknown` or `InlineCode`
3. Pipe targets are ALWAYS `Executed`
4. Command substitution content is ALWAYS `InlineCode`
5. Backtick content is ALWAYS `InlineCode`

## Performance Budget

- Classification adds <100μs to typical commands
- Zero allocations for simple commands (no pipes, no substitution)
- Early exit for commands that are obviously simple

## Acceptance Criteria

- Unit tests cover:
  - quoting and escaping
  - pipes and separators
  - command substitution and backticks
  - bash/python/node inline code flags
- Demonstrate the classifier allows documentation strings but still blocks real executions.
- Performance: classification adds <100μs to typical commands.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-07T20:03:13.621823546-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-08T00:31:56.328292556-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-08T00:31:56.328292556-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented execution-context classification module (src/context.rs): SpanKind enum (Executed, Argument, InlineCode, Data, HeredocBody, Unknown), Span/CommandSpans structures, shell tokenizer for quotes/pipes/substitution/inline-code detection (bash -c, python -c, etc.). 26 unit tests including performance verification (0.10μs/command, well under 100μs budget).  {"id":"git_safety_guard-t8x.4","title":"Optional: safe cleanup rules for common build dirs (reduce rm -rf friction)","description":"## Objective

Reduce real-world friction where users legitimately want to delete *known build/output directories* while keeping `rm -rf` protections strong.

## Problem

Today `core.filesystem` blocks `rm -rf` outside temp dirs. This is safe but can be disruptive for common workflows like:

- `rm -rf target/` (Rust)
- `rm -rf dist/` / `build/` (frontend)
- `rm -rf .next/` / `.turbo/` (framework caches)

Users often want to run these during development, and repeated blocks create pressure to disable the guard.

## Design options

### Option A (recommended): separate opt-in pack

- Add a `safe.cleanup` pack (disabled by default) that contains **safe patterns** allowing:
  - relative paths only
  - exact directory allowlist (no globs that can escape)
  - explicit rejection of `..` path traversal

### Option B: config allowlist for safe rm paths

- Allow users to configure a list of safe relative directories.
- More flexible but easier to misconfigure.

## Safety constraints (non-negotiable)

- Never allow absolute paths via this feature.
- Never allow `..` segments.
- Never allow an empty path or `/`.
- Prefer narrow, explicit matches.

## Acceptance criteria

- With the feature enabled, `rm -rf target/` is allowed.
- Commands like `rm -rf ../target/` and `rm -rf /home/user/target` remain blocked.
- Comprehensive unit tests cover path-traversal edge cases.

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-1gt.2` (rule-id allowlisting) may reduce the need for this, but this feature is still valuable because it is safer than allowlisting broad rm rules.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-t8x.5","title":"Optional: confidence scoring for ambiguous cases (warn vs deny)","description":"## Objective

Reduce unwarranted hard blocks in ambiguous situations by introducing a lightweight confidence model that can:

- trigger deeper analysis when needed
- downgrade to WARN (not DENY) when confidence is low

This is intentionally **late-stage** (P4): only attempt once correctness, determinism, and baseline false-positive immunity are solid.

## Background

Some matches are high-confidence catastrophes (`git reset --hard`, `rm -rf /`). Others are context-dependent and risk false positives.

A confidence model lets us avoid the worst UX failure mode:

- tool hard-blocks when it "isn't sure"

## Proposed design (v1)

- Define a small set of confidence signals:
  - match location: executed span vs data span
  - wrapper context: `echo`, `rg`, `git commit -m` (likely data)
  - presence of execution operators: `|`, `;`, `&&`, `$(...)`, backticks
  - heredoc presence (requires deeper scan)

- Evaluation flow:
  1) classify execution contexts
  2) run normal pack matching only on executed contexts
  3) if a match exists but confidence is below threshold:
     - run deeper analysis (tokenizer details, heredoc extraction/AST)
  4) if still low confidence:
     - WARN + log (do not DENY)

## Safety constraints

- Severity `error` rules still default DENY unless match is clearly only in data spans.

## Acceptance criteria

- Confidence scoring demonstrably reduces false-positive hard blocks in a curated corpus.
- Adds no measurable overhead to the fast path when confidence scoring is disabled.

## Tests

- Corpus-based tests comparing decisions with and without confidence scoring enabled.

## Dependencies

- `git_safety_guard-t8x.2` execution-context classification
- `git_safety_guard-1gt.3.1` severity taxonomy
- `git_safety_guard-2ta` tiered heredoc scanning (for deeper analysis)

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-tlog","title":"Add detailed tracing and logging for heredoc detection","description":"# Tracing and Logging Implementation

## Objective

Add comprehensive tracing using the `tracing` crate to enable debugging and monitoring of heredoc detection.

## Dependencies to Add

```toml
[dependencies]
tracing = "0.1"
tracing-subscriber = { version = "0.3", features = ["env-filter"] }
```

## Log Levels

- **ERROR**: parsing failures, timeout exceeded
- **WARN**: fallback triggered, unknown language
- **INFO**: heredoc detected, command blocked/allowed
- **DEBUG**: pattern matches, timing details
- **TRACE**: full command text, AST details (redacted/truncated as needed)

## Instrumentation Points

### 1. Main Check Flow

```rust
#[tracing::instrument(skip(cmd), fields(cmd_len = cmd.len()))]
pub fn check_command(cmd: &str) -> CheckResult {
    let start = Instant::now();

    // Quick reject
    if global_quick_reject(cmd) {
        tracing::trace!("quick_reject: no relevant keywords");
        return CheckResult::allowed();
    }

    // Heredoc detection
    if might_contain_heredoc(cmd) {
        tracing::debug!("heredoc_detected: triggering Tier 2 analysis");
        if let Some(result) = check_heredoc(cmd) {
            tracing::info!(
                blocked = result.blocked,
                reason = ?result.reason,
                elapsed_us = start.elapsed().as_micros(),
                "heredoc_check_complete"
            );
            return result;
        }
    }

    // Pattern matching...
}
```

### 2. Heredoc Analysis

```rust
#[tracing::instrument(skip(cmd))]
fn check_heredoc(cmd: &str) -> Option<CheckResult> {
    let extraction_start = Instant::now();
    let heredoc = extract_heredoc(cmd)?;
    tracing::debug!(
        extraction_us = extraction_start.elapsed().as_micros(),
        content_len = heredoc.content.len(),
        "heredoc_extracted"
    );

    let lang = detect_language(&heredoc);
    tracing::debug!(language = ?lang, "language_detected");

    let parse_start = Instant::now();
    let matches = match_patterns(&heredoc.content, lang);
    tracing::debug!(
        parse_us = parse_start.elapsed().as_micros(),
        match_count = matches.len(),
        "patterns_checked"
    );

    // ...
}
```

### 3. Error Handling

```rust
match parse_with_ast_engine(&content, lang) {
    Ok(ast) => { /* ... */ }
    Err(e) => {
        tracing::warn!(
            error = %e,
            language = ?lang,
            "ast_parse_failed: falling back to allow"
        );
        return None;  // Fail-open (hook)
    }
}
```

## Environment Variable Control

```bash
# Enable heredoc tracing
RUST_LOG=dcg::heredoc=debug dcg check

# Enable timing info only
RUST_LOG=dcg=info dcg check

# Full trace (very verbose)
RUST_LOG=dcg=trace dcg check
```

## JSON Structured Logging

For machine parsing:

```rust
tracing_subscriber::fmt()
    .json()
    .with_env_filter(EnvFilter::from_default_env())
    .init();
```

## Performance impact

- Logging disabled: near-zero overhead (static checks)
- INFO/DEBUG: bounded overhead per event
- TRACE: may include serialization; should be opt-in and redacted

## Acceptance Criteria

- When tracing is disabled, heredoc path performance is not meaningfully impacted.
- When enabled, logs include:
  - tier timings (trigger/extract/parse/match)
  - language chosen (or unknown)
  - rule attribution for matches
  - explicit markers when fail-open occurs (timeout/parse error)
- Logging is safe-by-default:
  - no unbounded command/contents output
  - redaction/truncation available
- Unit tests can capture logs deterministically for at least one positive and one error path.

+[package]
+name = "destructive_command_guard-fuzz"
+version = "0.0.0"
+publish = false
+edition = "2024"
+
+[package.metadata]
+cargo-fuzz = true
+
+[dependencies]
+libfuzzer-sys = "0.4"
+arbitrary = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
+serde_json = "1.0"
+
+[dependencies.destructive_command_guard]
+path = ".."
+
+# Fuzz target: full evaluator
+[[bin]]
+name = "fuzz_evaluate"
+path = "fuzz_targets/fuzz_evaluate.rs"
+test = false
+doc = false
+bench = false
+
+# Fuzz target: hook JSON input parsing
+[[bin]]
+name = "fuzz_hook_input"
+path = "fuzz_targets/fuzz_hook_input.rs"
+test = false
+doc = false
+bench = false
+
+# Fuzz target: context classifier (shell tokenizer)
+[[bin]]
+name = "fuzz_context"
+path = "fuzz_targets/fuzz_context.rs"
+test = false
+doc = false
+bench = false
+
+# Fuzz target: command normalization
+[[bin]]
+name = "fuzz_normalize"
+path = "fuzz_targets/fuzz_normalize.rs"
+test = false
+doc = false
+bench = false
